items.title: Talk on a podcast about psychology
items.transcript: Podcast Host: Did you see that new thriller movie that came out last week? I did and loved it. The action, the plot twists... I was totally captivated. Time just flew by. Not a single thought occurred to me that was unrelated to the movie. What I experienced is what psychologists call hard fascination. Hard fascination means intense focus and concentration. Whether it's TV programs, video games... hard fascination is all too easy to come by in this modern world. There's another type of fascination--soft fascination. There's still effortless attention, meaning that no special effort is required for you to stay focused, but there's still room for other thoughts. When I take a walk in the park and look at the flowers and trees, for example, I might be thinking in the back of my mind about my dinner plans. Now, one thing to know is hard fascination causes mental fatigue. The mind is so intensely focused that it gets tired fast. What follows mental fatigue? You might find yourself easily distracted, irritable, and stressed. Soft fascination, in contrast, engages a different part of the brain--the DMN, or Default Mode Network, which soothes the mind and helps combat mental fatigue. So next time you feel like your mind is on overload, turn off the TV, put down your phone. Take a walk, or simply sit and stare at the clouds.

questions.stem: What is the topic of the talk?

options.B: How to keep the mind focused
options.C: Two types of fascination

questions.stem: Why does the speaker mention a movie?

options.A: To compare different types of movies
options.B: To introduce a concept in psychology
options.C: To explain how movies affect emotions
options.D: To encourage listeners to watch more movies

questions.stem: What does the speaker say about her walk in the park?

options.A: It is similar to her experience watching a good movie.
options.B: Her mind has space for thoughts unrelated to nature.
options.C: She needs to put in special effort to stay focused on flowers and trees.
options.D: She gets mental fatigue from her mind engaging in hard fascination.

questions.stem: What does the speaker say about Default Mode Network?

options.A: It is involved in soft fascination.
options.B: It leads to irritability and stress.
options.C: It is easily tired from overuse.
options.D: Its effect is unknown to psychologists. In an actual test, the clock will show you how much time you have to complete each question. You can use Next move to the next question. You WILL NOT be able to return to previous questions. Choose the best response.

items.title: Talk in an environmental science class
items.transcript: Professor: An ecological footprint is a measure of the environmental impact of an individual, community, or country. It calculates the amount of natural resources consumed and the waste generated by human activities, usually expressed in global hectares. By comparing ecological footprints, we can understand how different lifestyles and practices contribute to resource depletion and environmental degradation. For instance, the ecological footprint of someone living in a developed country is typically larger than that of someone living in a developing country, mainly due to higher consumption levels and waste production. Factors such as energy use, transportation, food consumption, and housing play significant roles in determining the size of an ecological footprint. Understanding ecological footprints is crucial for promoting sustainability. It helps identify areas where changes can be made to reduce environmental impact. For example, if you consume locally produced foods, you'll likely reduce your ecological footprint because less energy will be used for transporting that food. Governments and organizations can use ecological footprint data to develop policies aimed at achieving sustainable development. I'll discuss some examples of that next.

questions.stem: What is the main topic of the talk?

options.A: Changes in consumption in societies over time
options.C: Environmentally damaging activities
options.D: The role of governments in sustainability

questions.stem: Why does the speaker mention developed and developing countries?

options.A: To contradict a theory related to the ecological footprint
options.B: To show that resource depletion can be similar regardless of lifestyle
options.C: To illustrate the usefulness of comparing ecological footprints
options.D: To point out that production efficiency reduces the ecological footprint

questions.stem: What does the speaker mention as a way that people can reduce their ecological footprint?

options.C: By using environmentally friendly forms of transportation

questions.stem: What will the speaker most likely discuss next?

options.A: How information about ecological footprints has been used in creating policies
options.B: How data about ecological footprints has been collected
options.C: Why the idea of ecological footprint is often misunderstood
options.D: Why sustainable development can be difficult to achieve Answer Key Question Answer Number 1 A 2 C 3 B 4 B 5 D 6 D 7 D 8 A 9 C 10 B 11 B 12 D 13 A 14 D 15 C 16 B 17 B 18 A Answer Key Question Answer Number 1 B 2 A 3 D 4 B 5 A 6 B 7 C 8 B 9 B 10 B 11 A 12 C 13 B 14 C 15 D 16 A

items.title: Talk in a physics class
items.transcript: Professor: Dark matter is a type of matter that doesn't emit, absorb, or reflect light, making it invisible to current instruments. Despite being unobservable directly, it's believed to make up about 27 percent of the universe's mass-energy content. How do scientists know dark matter exists? Well, all the stars in a galaxy rotate around the center of their galaxy. According to basic physics, the farther a star is from the center of a galaxy, the slower it should rotate. However, observations show that stars in the outer regions of galaxies rotate at unexpectedly high speeds. This anomaly suggests that a massive, unseen matter is exerting gravitational force, keeping the stars moving faster than expected. Dark matter is hugely important for the structure of the universe. Computer simulations show that galaxies wouldn't have formed the way they did if dark matter hadn't been around. But what does dark matter consist of? Well, there are different theories, so let's consider a couple of them.

questions.stem: What is the main topic of the talk?

options.B: Material in the universe that cannot be directly seen
options.C: The distribution of stars in galaxies

questions.stem: According to the speaker, how do scientists infer the existence of dark matter?

options.A: From the temperature of visible matter
options.B: From the amount of light in the universe
options.C: From the number of stars in galaxies
options.D: From the speed of stars' movement

questions.stem: Why does the speaker mention computer simulations?

options.A: To support the claim that dark matter plays an important role in the universe
options.B: To describe how the gravitational forces acting on stars were calculated
options.C: To highlight the anomalies in basic physics
options.D: To explain how the speeds of stars were measured

questions.stem: What will the speaker most likely discuss next?

options.A: Measurements of dark matter in galaxies
options.C: The anomalies in galaxy rotation caused by dark matter
options.D: The distribution of dark matter in the universe In an actual test, the clock will show you how much time you have to complete each question. You can use Next move to the next question. You WILL NOT be able to return to previous questions. Choose the best response.

options.C: It's crowded.

options.C: I can't help it.

questions.stem: Man: Let's go hiking this weekend.

options.C: Have you checked the weather?

items.title: Talk in an environmental science class
items.transcript: Professor: Biomimicry is the human practice of learning from and imitating things found in nature in order to create new inventions. This way, scientists and engineers can create innovative solutions that are both efficient and sustainable. One well-known example of biomimicry is the development of a widely used fastening system. The inventor was inspired by the way burrs, uh thorns, from plants stuck to his dog's fur. By replicating the tiny hooks found on the burrs he removed from the dog fur, he created the new fastening system. Another example is the design of energy-efficient buildings based on the ventilation system of structures created by termites. Termites are ground-dwelling insects that use different materials to create hills, or mounds, in which the termites live. Termite mounds have a complex network of vents. The vents maintain a stable internal temperature, which can be emulated in human architecture. Biomimicry can also be applied to renewable energy. For instance, researchers are developing wind turbine blades modeled after the flippers of humpback whales. The unique shape of the flippers reduces drag and increases efficiency, leading to more effective wind energy capture.

questions.stem: What is the talk mainly about?

options.A: Ways in which humans use natural materials in clothing and architecture
options.C: New methods of using renewable energy
options.D: Examples of how humans imitate nature for practical purposes

questions.stem: Why does the speaker mention a dog?

options.A: To illustrate how an inventor copied animal behavior
options.B: To compare its efficiency in movement with that of humpback whales
options.C: To explain how a structure found in plants inspired an invention
options.D: To show the close bond between scientists and animals

questions.stem: What point does the speaker make about termite mounds?

options.A: The temperature inside of them does not change much.
options.B: Termites sometimes build them near human-made structures.
options.C: Building them takes a lot of energy.
options.D: They are created mostly from plant material.

questions.stem: What does the speaker imply about wind turbine blades that are being developed?

options.A: The noise they create can be harmful to whales.
options.B: They can be easily installed on buildings.
options.C: They can be efficiently constructed from renewable materials.
options.D: They are very similar to some whales' flippers in shape. Answer Key Question Answer Number 1 B 2 B 3 D 4 A 5 A 6 A 7 A 8 D 9 C 10 C 11 C 12 B 13 C 14 A 15 B 16 D 17 A 18 B Answer Key Question Answer Number 1 B 2 C 3 C 4 A 5 C 6 B 7 A 8 C 9 B 10 A 11 D 12 A 13 D 14 C 15 A 16 D
